article_title: Chrome was writing a lot more to my SSD than I thought — I fixed it
core_thesis: Google Chrome performs excessive background writes to SSDs due to constant cache updates, session restore data, and background processes, which can be mitigated by capping cache size, relocating cache to a secondary drive or RAM disk, and disabling unnecessary background activity.
detailed_summary: The author noticed constant disk activity spikes on their SSD and used Windows Resource Monitor to identify Chrome as a top writer, averaging ~1 MB/s and 900 million bytes per session during normal browsing. Chrome's cache folder contained 858 files totaling 220MB, and the browser continuously rewrites cache as sites update assets. The primary fix involves appending command-line flags to Chrome's shortcut: `--disk-cache-size=104857600` to limit cache to 100MB, and `--disk-cache-dir="D:\ChromeCache"` to relocate cache to a secondary drive (HDD, SSD, or RAM disk). After applying these tweaks, writes dropped to 12–80 KB/s during light tasks, and further to ~9 KB/s after relocation. Additional steps include disabling background apps when Chrome is closed, setting preload pages to Standard, and removing unused extensions. The article also notes that session restore data (not just cache) is a major contributor to writes, as highlighted by a commenter who moved their entire profile to an Intel Optane drive for better endurance.

entities.name: Afam Onyimadu
entities.role: Author of the article, tech journalist

entities.name: Google Chrome
entities.role: Web browser causing excessive SSD writes

entities.name: Windows Resource Monitor
entities.role: Tool used to identify disk write sources

entities.name: Intel Optane
entities.role: High-endurance storage solution suggested by a commenter

key_data_points: Chrome wrote ~1 MB/s to the SSD during normal browsing, totaling 900 million bytes per session (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: understanding disk I/O patterns can help in forensic analysis or detecting exfiltration).
Chrome's cache folder contained 858 files and 220MB, constantly rewritten as sites update assets (🟢 relevant to homelab: cache management is critical for SSD longevity in servers).
Limiting cache to 100MB via `--disk-cache-size=104857600` reduced writes to 12–80 KB/s during light tasks (🟢 relevant to programming: command-line flags can be automated in scripts for deployment).
Relocating cache to a secondary drive or RAM disk further reduced writes to ~9 KB/s (🟢 relevant to homelab: RAM disks can be used for temporary data to reduce SSD wear).
Disabling 'Continue running background apps when Chrome is closed' and setting preload pages to Standard reduces background writes (🟢 relevant to productivity: reduces system resource usage).

Session restore data writes multiple times per second, especially with many tabs open, and can be mitigated by moving the entire profile to a high-endurance drive like Intel Optane (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: session data persistence can be a forensic artifact).
methodology_steps: Open Windows Resource Monitor (resmon.exe) and navigate to the Disk tab. Sort by the Write column to identify top writing processes.
Right-click Chrome's shortcut, select Properties, and append `--disk-cache-size=104857600` to the Target field to limit cache to 100MB.
Optionally, add `--disk-cache-dir="D:\ChromeCache"` (or another path) to relocate cache to a secondary drive or RAM disk. Ensure the folder exists.
Restart Chrome and clear existing cache to enforce the new limits.
In Chrome settings, disable 'Continue running background apps when Chrome is closed' and set 'Preload pages' to Standard.
Remove unused extensions to reduce background writes from filter lists or logs.
conclusions: Chrome's constant cache updates and session restore data cause significant SSD writes, but capping cache size and relocating it to a secondary drive or RAM disk effectively reduces write frequency without noticeable performance degradation.
Additional tweaks like disabling background apps and trimming extensions further minimize writes, though the primary impact comes from cache management.

For users with many tabs, session restore data is a major contributor; moving the entire Chrome profile to a high-endurance drive (e.g., Intel Optane) or reducing save frequency can extend SSD lifespan.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as SSD wear from browser caching can affect server longevity and performance. The command-line flags and relocation techniques can be scripted for automated deployment across multiple machines. For Red Teaming, understanding Chrome's disk I/O patterns helps in forensic analysis or detecting data exfiltration via cache. As a programmer, you can apply similar caching strategies to your own applications to reduce disk writes. The productivity tips (disabling background apps, trimming extensions) also help keep your development environment lean.
extra_advice: Consider creating a PowerShell script that applies these Chrome flags and cache relocation automatically when deploying new systems. For homelab, you could set up a RAM disk for browser cache on servers with ample RAM to eliminate SSD writes entirely. Also, explore Chromium-based browsers like Brave or Ungoogled Chromium that may offer built-in controls for disk writes.
